edges.description: Loss of all saposin cofactors impairs degradation of multiple sphingolipids.
edges.source: Prosaposin Deficiency and Loss of All Saposins

edges.target: Prosaposin Deficiency and Loss of All Saposins

nodes.description: Without any saposin cofactors, multiple sphingolipids accumulate simultaneously in lysosomes across the nervous system and viscera, and loss of intact prosaposin (which is itself essential for neuronal survival) compounds the neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Combined saposin deficiency: A rare occurrence.
evidence.snippet: Combined saposin deficiency (OMIM #611721), an exceedingly rare lysosomal storage disorder, is caused by a mutation in the gene PSAP.

meta.association: Biallelic null PSAP variants abolishing prosaposin and all saposins

evidence.reference_title: Combined saposin deficiency: A rare occurrence.
evidence.snippet: Combined saposin deficiency (OMIM #611721), an exceedingly rare lysosomal storage disorder, is caused by a mutation in the gene PSAP.

evidence.reference_title: A novel mutation in the coding region of the prosaposin gene leads to a complete deficiency of prosaposin and saposins, and is associated with a complex sphingolipidosis dominated by lactosylceramide accumulation.
evidence.snippet:
The patient was homozygous for a 1 bp deletion (c.803delG) within
the SAP-B domain of the prosaposin gene which leads to a frameshift and
premature stop codon

nodes.description: Biallelic null PSAP variants abolish the prosaposin precursor, eliminating all four saposins (A-D). Each saposin is an essential cofactor for a distinct lysosomal sphingolipid hydrolase, so their combined loss simultaneously impairs multiple degradation pathways.
nodes.id: Prosaposin Deficiency and Loss of All Saposins

evidence.reference_title: Combined saposin deficiency: A rare occurrence.
evidence.snippet: This gene encodes a protein, prosaposin, that cleaves into four constituent proteins, each of which has a role as a cofactor for the enzymes whose deficiency results in Krabbe disease, metachromatic leukodystrophy, Gaucher disease, and Farber disease, respectively.
